name: lemony shrimp and bean stew
instructions:
From NYT Cooking. A fast one-pot shrimp-and-white-bean stew — leek base + cannellini + stock, with shrimp marinated in lemon zest + paprika + garlic + salt + pepper and cooked separately, then folded back in at the end. Ready in ~20 minutes.

INGREDIENTS (4 servings):
- 1 tsp fresh lemon zest + 2 tbsp lemon juice
- 1 tsp sweet or smoked paprika (either works — see notes)
- 2 garlic cloves, grated
- kosher salt and black pepper
- 1 lb peeled, deveined large shrimp (tails removed)
- 4 tbsp unsalted butter (1/2 stick)
- 2 large leeks (trimmed, halved lengthwise, white and light green parts sliced crosswise 1/2-inch thick) — or 1 large onion, minced
- 1 (15-oz) can cannellini beans (or other white beans), rinsed
- 2 cups chicken stock or vegetable stock
- 2 tbsp finely chopped fresh parsley (optional)
- toasted bread, for serving (optional)

PROCEDURE:
1. MARINATE THE SHRIMP: combine lemon zest, paprika, grated garlic, 3/4 tsp salt, and 3/4 tsp pepper in a medium bowl. Add shrimp and toss to coat.
2. COOK THE SHRIMP, THEN REMOVE: in a large pot, melt butter over medium-high heat. When butter is foaming, add shrimp and cook, stirring occasionally, until pink and starting to curl (2-3 min). Transfer with a slotted spoon to a plate; set aside. This is deliberate — shrimp goes in and out fast to avoid overcooking (see notes).
3. SAUTE THE LEEKS: add the leeks to the same pot, season with salt and pepper, and cook over medium until soft and starting to brown on the edges (4-5 min), stirring occasionally. Butter-fond from the shrimp stays in the pot.
4. SIMMER THE BEANS: add beans and stock, bring to a boil over high, lower heat, and simmer 8-10 min.
5. FINISH OFF HEAT: stir in the reserved shrimp (plus any juices from the plate), parsley, and lemon juice. Season with salt and pepper to taste. Serve with toasted bread.
notes:
=== HEADNOTE ===

NYT framing: minimal prep and a quick cook time; elegant-feeling for a weeknight meal. Explicitly variable — substitute equal amounts of flaky white fish or seared scallops for the shrimp, or stretch it to serve 6 by stirring in more butter and serving over cooked spaghetti or rigatoni. A good glug of finishing olive oil also welcome.

=== THE LOAD-BEARING TECHNIQUE — COOK SHRIMP SEPARATELY, ADD BACK AT THE END ===

COOK THE SHRIMP FIRST, REMOVE, AND FOLD BACK IN AT THE END: this is the whole trick of the recipe. Shrimp go rubbery and flavorless the moment they're cooked past pink-and-just-curled — a two-to-three minute window. By cooking them briefly upfront, pulling them out with a slotted spoon, and only folding them back in OFF the simmer at the very end, the shrimp stay tender throughout the whole cook.

The reserved 'juices on the plate' from the resting shrimp also get folded back in at the end — that liquid carries lemon-and-paprika-forward marinade concentrate and is real flavor, not garnish.

DO NOT cook the shrimp in the simmering stew (they'd stay in too long) and do not put pre-cooked shrimp into a hot simmer (they'd reheat past done). The off-heat fold is what preserves the tender texture the whole dish depends on.

=== MAKE-AHEAD RULE (VIA READER TIP) — SHRIMP DAY-OF ONLY ===

Reader tip captured: shrimp will turn rubbery and flavorless if cooked ahead and reheated. To make ahead:
- Prep the marinade (lemon zest + paprika + garlic + salt + pepper) but DON'T add the shrimp yet.
- Cook the leek + bean stew and hold it in the fridge (cooled, covered).
- Both keep fine.
- Just before serving, reheat the stew, THEN marinate + cook the shrimp fresh in butter, then fold them into the stew.
This lets the recipe scale into meal-prep territory without losing what makes it work. Marinated raw shrimp in the fridge also isn't recommended — the acid from the lemon zest + garlic will start to 'cook' the shrimp texturally if left too long (ceviche-adjacent).

=== SWEET OR SMOKED PAPRIKA — EITHER WORKS, DIFFERENT REGISTER ===

The recipe explicitly allows either sweet or smoked paprika, and they push the dish in different directions:
- SWEET PAPRIKA: keeps the dish light, brighter, lemon-and-butter-forward. Reads more coastal-mediterranean.
- SMOKED PAPRIKA: adds a background campfire depth against the shrimp and butter. Reads more spanish / gambas-adjacent.
Both are correct. Pick based on what you want the finished dish to taste like.

=== LEEKS VS ONION — LEEKS ARE THE DEFAULT ===

Recipe allows 1 large minced onion as a substitute for 2 leeks, but the leeks are the preferred call. Leeks give a soft green sweetness that pairs cleanly with shrimp; minced yellow onion reads more assertive and can push the dish toward a heavier stew register. Use leeks if you have them.

=== BUTTER IS NON-NEGOTIABLE — 4 TABLESPOONS IS THE FLOOR ===

Unsalted butter is the fat here and 4 tablespoons is the minimum. Reader/NYT commentary suggests adding MORE butter (up to 6 tbsp) when stretching to feed six with pasta. This is a butter-forward dish; the leeks and shrimp both want the butter as the fat medium, and reducing it to 'be lighter' drops the whole thing flat. Use unsalted specifically so you can control the salt precisely alongside the marinade + stock salt.

=== PROTEIN VARIATIONS ===

RECIPE-ENDORSED SUBSTITUTIONS (from the headnote): flaky white fish or seared scallops, equal amount. Both keep the seafood-forward identity. White fish (cod, halibut) would go in as small chunks and cook briefly in the simmer at the end; scallops would be seared separately (same technique as shrimp) and folded back in.

=== STRETCH TO SIX WITH PASTA ===

NYT-endorsed stretch: stir in additional butter and serve over cooked spaghetti or rigatoni. This turns the dish from a bowl-of-stew into a pasta-with-shrimp-and-beans, and the extra butter is real (helps the sauce coat the pasta). If going this route, cook the pasta separately and toss the finished stew (with the extra butter melted in) with the drained pasta right before serving.

=== FINISHING OLIVE OIL DRIZZLE (OPTIONAL) ===

NYT-endorsed: 'a good glug of your best olive oil would also be welcome' as a finish. Reach for a peppery finishing olive oil (not the neutral cooking oil), drizzle over the served bowls. Pairs with the parsley + lemon juice at the end.

=== NO GREENS, NO TOMATOES, NO CREAM ===

RESIST DEPTH-INGREDIENTS: this is a spare recipe on purpose. The shrimp + leeks + white beans + stock + butter + lemon axis is what gives the dish its clean, coastal-mediterranean identity. Adding greens (spinach, kale) turns it into a heartier all-in-one stew and dilutes the shrimp-and-lemon focus. Adding tomatoes pushes it toward cioppino / italian-fish-stew territory (a real, different dish). Adding cream makes it a chowder. All valid dishes, none this one. If you want any of those directions, cook that dish specifically — don't hedge.

=== WHITE WINE IS CONSPICUOUSLY ABSENT FROM THE ORIGINAL — ADD IT ===

The NYT recipe as written doesn't call for white wine, which is genuinely weird — shrimp + leeks + butter + lemon + garlic is exactly the flavor stack that classic french and italian coastal cooking builds around a white-wine deglaze. Its absence here reads more like an omission than a deliberate choice.

WHERE TO ADD IT: after cooking the shrimp and pulling them out (step 2), and after sautéing the leeks until soft (step 3), splash in a generous pour of white wine (~1/2 cup) and let it reduce briefly before adding the beans and stock. The wine deglazes the buttery-shrimp-fond stuck to the pot bottom and gives the finished broth a proper acidic-aromatic backbone that pure lemon-at-the-end can't provide alone. Lemon juice at the finish still goes in as written.

WHY THE RECIPE PROBABLY LEFT IT OUT: my guess is minimalism-for-a-weeknight — one fewer ingredient to buy, one fewer step. But the recipe is already using stock and butter and lemon; adding wine isn't a real complexity increase, and the flavor payoff is significant. Consider it a canonical addition rather than a variation.

Secondary rather than primary because the original NYT recipe doesn't include it and I'm departing from the source. But if I were writing this recipe from scratch (not adapting from NYT) white wine would probably be primary.

=== SIBLING RECIPES ===

- creamy-italian-white-beans: the cream-and-breadcrumbs sibling white-bean side. Fast, no protein.
- cheesy-white-bean-tomato-bake: the tomato-paste-and-mozzarella sibling white-bean bake. Fast, no protein.
- diablo-shrimp: the spicy-tomato shrimp treatment. Different flavor axis but shrimp-forward.
